free_text: Excitation Functions For Proton-Induced Reactions
With Copper
.Cyclotron Laboratory
Irradiations were Performed with the Internal Scattered
Beam with 180 Degree Focusing in the Cyclotron Magnetic
Field. Details of this Method Resulting in a Better
Energy Resolution are Given by N.M.Hintz Et Al., Phys.
The Observed Counting Rates were Corrected For Dead
Time, Background Activity, Absorption, Self-Absorption,
Scattering and Self-Scattering. Not Included were
Corrections Due to Different Backing Materials. These
May Increase the Values Presented Here by About
No Errors are Given by the Authors.
Lapf
Reaction Lines are Corrected. Lower Case.
Last checking has been done.
The Slight Peak at About 80 MeV is Probably Caused
By the Cu-63(P,4N+P)Cu-59 Reaction.
Cu-Oxide Powder Containing Separated Cu-63 Enclosed in
Flat Al-Capsules. Thickness of Powder Layer About
0.25 mm. The Stack Consisted of 4 to 8 Capsules
Interspaced with Al-Absorbers.
According to Hintz Et Al.,Phys.Rev.88,19,1952 the
Energy at Each Target Foil was Calculated from the
Average Radius and Magnetic Field For the Scattered
Protons Together with Range Energy Curves Given by
W.A.Aron,B.G.Hoffman and F.C.Williams, Ucrl-121(REV.)
Initial Proton Energies 100 and 75 MeV, Respectively.
The Copper Oxide was Counted Through a 370 Mg/Cm**2
Al-Absorber to Stop any C-11 Activity Formed from
The Oxygen. The Relative Yield of Cu-62 was Determined
From a Decay Curve Analysis. Absolute Cross Sections
Were Obtained by Comparing the Relative Yields of
Cu-62 (10.5 MIN) For the Cu-63(P,N+P)Cu-62 Reaction
With Cu-62 Measured in Equilibrium with Zn-62 (9.5 HR)
For the Cu-63(P,2N)Zn-62 Reaction at 26 MeV (SEE
B0054005). The Radiation of Zn-62 was Suppressed by
The Al-Absorber.
Used As Secondary Monitor

In a Recent Compilation by Colle Et Al. The Present
Data are Compared to Later Measurements and Found
To be Systematically Higher Than All the Others.

Newton Compared these Data to His Own and Other
Previous Measurements and Found Meadows Values
Systematically Higher. He Concluded That this Might be
Due to Poor Energy Resolution, Due to Use of the
Stacked-Foil Technique, and Difficulties in Activity
Measurements.
